mappings.rationaleAI: Laws of nature from Thales through Newton to string theory: philosophical status of scientific theory (B.5.a) and formal structures (B.3.c).
Magnetism from Greek lodestone through Gilbert's De Magnete: transition from natural philosophy to systematic investigation.
Hume's scepticism about causation and induction: foundational challenges to scientific epistemology.
Galileo's telescope and Kepler's optics: interplay between instruments and theory in the scientific revolution.
Bacon's induction, Popper's falsifiability, paradigm debates: core philosophy of science from 17th century onward.
Quantum measurement problem: philosophical responses to changes in natural science.
Evolving social role of the scientist from natural philosopher to professional researcher.
Geology's emergence as a discipline from ancient speculation to rigorous science.
Chaos theory challenged Newtonian predictability, contributing to debates about determinism.
Scientific explanation vs poetic wonder: two cultures divide.
9th-century translation of Greek scientific texts into Arabic: transmission of science between civilizations.
Genetics from Mendel through Modern Synthesis to DNA: 19th-20th century biological progress.
Quest for grand unified theory: drive toward unification in 20th-century physics.
Pliny's Natural History: Roman approach to systematic knowledge compilation.
Pythagorean doctrine as foundational Greek protoscience bridging observation and metaphysics.
Roger Bacon: medieval advocacy of empirical observation anticipating Baconian method.
12th-century transmission of Arabic-translated Aristotle into Latin scholarship.
Encyclopedie systematized Enlightenment knowledge, challenging religious authority.
Needham Question: why modern science emerged in Europe rather than China.
Baconian inductive method: foundation of modern empirical science.
Kepler's laws transformed astronomy, exemplifying the scientific revolution.
Dalton's atomic theory from Quaker dissenting culture and provincial science.
Kinetic theory development: molecular existence debated, scientific realism questions.
20th-century science: paradigm theory, falsifiability, specialization, postmodern critiques.
Antikythera Mechanism: Hellenistic scientific and engineering capability.
British Enlightenment empiricism and natural philosophy.
Environmental determinism debate: scientific vs humanistic explanation.
Galen's systematic physiological theory: Greco-Roman scientific method.
Renaissance recovery of Greek mathematics transforming natural philosophy.

mappings.episodeTitle: Darwin: On the Origins of Charles Darwin
mappings.rationaleAI: Darwin's intellectual formation in early 19th-century institutional context.

Alchemy's transformation from proto-chemistry to influencing the scientific revolution, including Newton's alchemical work informing his physics, is central to the history and philosophy of science (A.1 ancient and medieval science, A.2 the Scientific Revolution).

The episode addresses the methodology of science from Galen's anatomical studies to modern experimental practice, engaging with the history (A.1) and philosophy of science (B) regarding empirical methods and ethical limits.

Darwin's continuing research after Origin represents a major chapter in 19th-century science, with his methodology and extensive publications exemplifying the difference in styles of research and progress in biology (A.6).

Hodgkin's Nobel Prize-winning work exemplifies 20th-century science (A.7) and her Cold War peace activism connects to contemporary problems of the moral and political difficulties facing science (A.8).

Du Chatelet's translation of Newton's Principia and her own Institutions de Physique contributed to the results of the new philosophy including establishment of scientific societies and progress in particular fields (A.4.b.ii).
The verification principle and the Circle's rejection of metaphysics in favor of empirical verification directly shaped the philosophy of science.
Galileo's assertion that the universe is written in mathematical language connects to the philosophy of science and the relationship between mathematics and natural science.
